docs.title: JWY's blog
docs.text: My main interests are deep learning, quantitative trading and high performance computing. I'm always open to learning new things!

docs.title: First Post
docs.text:
Finally deployed not so sketchy blog.

I have tried bunch of solutions, from only using html, css, and javascript to using production grade frameworks such as Next.js to create my own blog. They all failed because it took long time to build and I simply don't have the time and dedication to build it. I also believe I should focus on the writing not building. Not that building website is fun, but I get carried away. Hopefully I can finally write some posts.

For those who are starting out, mkdocs material is simple but powerful way to build documentation, blog and more.

docs.title: Pkl language
docs.text:
There are many configuration languages such as json, yaml and toml. They are perfect for simple tasks but quickly get out of hand when it is used for seting up complicated systems like docker or kubernetes. Then Pkl language from Apple came to rescue. Here is the link to the home page. (Don't confuse this with Python's pickle.)

Biggest advantages of Pkl are - validations - import of other files - lazy evaluation

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
name = "Pkl: Configure your Systems in New Ways"
attendants = 100
isInteractive = true
amountLearned = 13.37

$ pkl eval -f json /Users/me/tutorial/intro.pkl
output: 
{
  "name": "Pkl: Configure your Systems in New Ways",
  "attendants": 100,
  "isInteractive": true,
  "amountLearned": 13.37
}

docs.title: Pkl-Python
docs.text: I am a heavy user of Python and needed binding to pkl. Since pkl is very new, I needed to make my own. Here is the link to the github page

docs.title: Quick Introduction
docs.text:
Install with pip install pkl-python

import pkl
config = pkl.load("path/to/pkl/file.pkl")
